does: This paper develops POLARIS, a training recipe for enabling small language models to generate long-form creative stories that maintain quality while respecting length constraints. The method combines reinforcement learning from human feedback using GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) with two key innovations: using a frontier LLM as a judge with a structured story-quality rubric to provide online rewards, and human-reference injection (HRI) where teacher-forced human-written stories anchor each GRPO group as high-reward examples. Applied to Qwen 3.5-9B using 1.4K prompt-story pairs from literary anthologies and trained on 4 A100 GPUs, POLARIS-9B achieves competitive performance with much larger models across five benchmarks while more faithfully following length instructions, with human evaluation confirming preference over the base model.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in aligning smaller models through reward-based learning, extending beyond standard supervised fine-tuning to handle the specific challenges of long-form generation. It connects to prior research on using LLM-as-a-judge approaches for evaluation and reward modeling, combining these with group-based policy optimization methods like GRPO that have shown promise for efficient training. The paper also engages with work on training data quality and curriculum learning by leveraging curated literary anthologies rather than web-scale data, and contributes to the broader effort to democratize capable text generation by pushing open-weight models toward frontier model capabilities in specialized domains like creative writing.
enables: This work opens several directions for improving small-model capabilities in long-form generation. The structured rubric approach and LLM-as-judge framework could generalize to other long-sequence tasks like scientific writing, technical documentation, or dialogue. The human-reference injection technique suggests broader possibilities for using expert demonstrations more effectively in RLHF-style training. The efficiency gains from training on small models with modest compute demonstrate a path toward making specialized creative writing capability more accessible, potentially enabling further research into personalized story generation, genre-specific fine-tuning, or interactive story co-authoring systems where users can iterate with capable smaller models rather than relying solely on frontier APIs.
